Last updated: 2026-04-13

# CyodaLight — Complete Site Content

## What is CyodaLight?

CyodaLight is an open-source entity database management system (EDBMS) and workflow runtime. You define your entity types — the fields they carry, the states they can be in, and the valid transitions between states. CyodaLight enforces those rules at the API boundary, records every state change immutably, and lets you query the history of any entity at any point in time.

It is written in Go and distributed as a single binary with no distributed cluster required.

CyodaLight supports two storage modes:

- **In-memory mode**: Stores entity state in process memory. Zero external dependencies. Starts in under 2 seconds. Resets when the process stops. Use for development and fast test cycles.
- **PostgreSQL mode**: Stores entity state durably in a PostgreSQL database. Persists across restarts. Use for integration testing and local validation with real persistence.

## What is an EDBMS?

An entity database management system (EDBMS) is a runtime that manages the full lifecycle of typed, stateful business objects — entities. Where a standard database stores rows, an EDBMS tracks state transitions, enforces lifecycle rules, and maintains a temporal audit trail. CyodaLight adds a workflow engine to this model: transition rules are explicit, invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary, and the complete state history is queryable.

## Key Capabilities

- **Stateful entities**: Define entity types with typed fields, valid states, and transition rules. CyodaLight enforces the model at runtime.
- **Lifecycle enforcement**: Finite state machine engine. Invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary — no defensive code required in your application.
- **Temporal history**: Every state change is recorded. Query the exact state of any entity at any point in time.
- **Immutable audit trail**: Non-modifiable record of all transitions: what changed, from which state, to which state, and when.
- **In-memory mode**: Zero external dependencies. Starts in under 2 seconds. Resets cleanly between test runs.
- **PostgreSQL mode**: Durable, transactional entity storage. State persists across restarts for local integration testing.
- **gRPC API**: Language-agnostic API surface. Client libraries for Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin. Any gRPC-capable language works.
- **Test harness**: Reset entity state between tests with a single API call. No teardown scripts, no manual cleanup.

## Supported Languages

Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin via gRPC client libraries. Any language with a gRPC implementation can connect to the CyodaLight API directly.

## Install and Run

Download the latest release from GitHub:
https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/releases

### In-Memory Mode (recommended for getting started)
```bash
cyodalight start --mode=memory
# HTTP API:  http://localhost:8080
# gRPC:      localhost:9090
```

### PostgreSQL Mode
```bash
cyodalight start --mode=postgres \
  --postgres-url=postgres://localhost:5432/cyoda
```

### Docker
```bash
docker run -p 8080:8080 -p 9090:9090 \
  ghcr.io/cyoda-platform/cyodalight:latest \
  --mode=memory
```

## Hello World Example (Go)

The example below creates a Greeting entity in the PENDING state, then drives a lifecycle transition based on the hour of day. Import paths are illustrative — see docs.cyoda.net for the published client libraries.

```go
package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "time"
    cyoda "github.com/cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/client"
)

func main() {
    client, _ := cyoda.NewClient("localhost:9090")
    ctx := context.Background()

    entity, _ := client.CreateEntity(ctx, &cyoda.Entity{
        Type:  "Greeting",
        State: "PENDING",
        Data:  map[string]any{"hour": time.Now().Hour()},
    })

    if time.Now().Hour() < 12 {
        client.Transition(ctx, entity.ID, "TO_MORNING")
        fmt.Println("Good morning")
    } else {
        client.Transition(ctx, entity.ID, "TO_AFTERNOON")
        fmt.Println("Good afternoon")
    }
}
```

## Development to Production Path

1. **CyodaLight — In-Memory**: Start here. Define your entity model, run the workflow engine, iterate on your API logic.
2. **CyodaLight — PostgreSQL**: Switch to durable storage. Run your integration tests against the same entity model and API.
3. **Cyoda Cloud**: The managed, distributed option — horizontally scalable, multi-node. Same gRPC API. No application changes needed.

## FAQ

**What is CyodaLight?**
CyodaLight is an open-source entity database management system (EDBMS) and workflow runtime. You define your entity types, the valid states each entity can be in, and the transition rules between states. CyodaLight enforces those rules, records every state change, and lets you query the history of any entity at any point in time. It runs as a single binary with no distributed cluster required.

**What is an EDBMS?**
An entity database management system (EDBMS) is a runtime that manages the full lifecycle of typed, stateful business objects — entities. Where a standard database stores rows, an EDBMS tracks state transitions, enforces lifecycle rules, and maintains a temporal audit trail. CyodaLight adds a workflow engine to this model: transition rules are explicit, invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary, and the complete state history is queryable.

**Is it free and open source?**
Yes. CyodaLight is released under the Apache 2.0 License. There are no usage limits, no telemetry, and no account required to run it.

**What is the difference between in-memory and PostgreSQL mode?**
In-memory mode stores entity state in process memory. It has zero external dependencies and resets when the process stops — useful for development and fast test cycles. PostgreSQL mode stores entity state durably in a PostgreSQL database, persisting across restarts. Use PostgreSQL mode for integration testing or when you need state to survive a process restart.

**Who is CyodaLight for?**
Backend developers building stateful, event-driven, or workflow-driven systems who need a local runtime that enforces business rules and tracks state history. Teams replacing hand-rolled state machines and audit log tables. Developers who want a clean EDBMS API with no cloud dependency for local development and testing.

**What languages are supported?**
Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin via gRPC client libraries. Any language with a gRPC implementation can connect to the CyodaLight API directly.

**Can I deploy to production with the same code?**
Yes. CyodaLight exposes the same gRPC API as Cyoda Cloud — the managed, distributed production option built by the Cyoda team. Applications developed against CyodaLight run against Cyoda Cloud without API changes. Point the client at a different host; nothing else changes.

**Where is the documentation?**
Full documentation is at docs.cyoda.net. The CyodaLight quickstart guide covers installation, entity definition, and your first workflow in under ten minutes.

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go
- Releases: https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/releases
- Documentation: https://docs.cyoda.net
- Quick Start: https://docs.cyoda.net/cyodalight/quickstart
- Cyoda Cloud: https://cyoda.com
